coredata.dc:title: A photoluminescence sensor for in-situ monitoring of the dopamine neurotransmitters released from PC12 cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qaisi, Ramy M.
Akhdhar, Abdullah
Choi, Jeong-woo
Ahmed El-Said, Waleed
coredata.dc:description: Constructing simple, stable, fast, and sensitive neurotransmitter-based sensors is a promising tool to diagnose neurological diseases. Dopamine (DA), “a catecholamine neurotransmitter” is important in transmitting nerve impulses. Therefore, great attention is taken to monitor DA concentrations received. The challenge in developing a DA-based sensor is to enhance its stability and sensitivity. Thus, we have used o-phthalaldehyde (OPA)/2-mercapto ethanol (2ME)/mesoporous silica instated of 2ME in solution. Here we have successfully developed a fluorescence DA neurotransmitters sensor. The sensor was used for detecting a wide range of concentrations of DA (5 nM to 5 µM). Effects of pH (4.3–11.4) and temperatures (25–70 °C) on the sensor efficiency were investigated. The detection limit was 1.35 × 10−11 mol/dm3, which is lower than the normal DA level in the central nervous system. The results indicated that using OPA/2ME/MSNPs has long-time stability over a year of its preparation. Moreover, the developed sensor showed high specificity towards DA in the presence of different interferences such as ascorbic acid or another catecholamine neurotransmitter such as γ-aminobutyric acid. Finally, the fabricated biosensor was used to monitor the DA neurotransmitter released from PC12 cells. Hence, it was successfully developed a simple and stable probe for accurate photoluminescence detection of DA neurotransmitters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optical biosensor
Dopamine neurotransmitter
UV–vis
PC12 cells
Silica/o-phthalaldehyde/2-mercapto ethanol (2-ME) composite
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the optical DA sensor
DA sensing and real sample application
Sensitivity and specificity of dopamine neurotransmitter monitoring using MSNPs/OPA/2ME sensor based on UV–vis technique
Photoluminescence determination of DA using MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor
The limits of detection of DA neurotransmitters and the interaction mechanism
The durability of the developed sensor
Monitoring of dopamine released from PC12 cells as a model of real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work has been funded by the Deputyship for Research& Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia through project number MOE-IF-20-03-1. Therefore, the authors gratefully acknowledge technical and financial support from the Ministry of Education and the University of Jeddah. This research was supported by the Brain Pool program funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT through the National Research Foundation of Korea (171S-4-3-1800).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of MSNPs, (b) TEM image of MSNPs, and (c) FTIR spectra of MSNPs before and after functionalization with OPA/2ME.

(a) UV–vis spectra of DA in PBS before and after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, (b) Raman spectrum of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, (c) UV–vis spectra of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME in different pH solutions, (d) photo images of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME in different pH solutions, (e) photo images of DA in the presence of different pH solutions, and (f) UV–vis spectra of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME at different temperatures,

(a) UV–vis spectra of different concentrations (50 nmol/L –5 µmol/L) of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, (b) the relationship between the intensities absorption bands with the DA concentrations, (c) UV–vis spectra of different concentrations of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME in the presence of 3 µM AA, and (d) UV–vis spectra of MSNPs/OPA/2ME after interacting with DA (black curve), GABA (red curve), and NE (blue curve).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, (b) relationship between emission intensity and the DA concentrations, (c) photo images of different concentrations of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, and (d) Adsorption isotherm for the adsorption of DA on MSNPs/OPA-2ME at optimal experimental conditions. The nonlinear form of the Langmuir-Freundlich isotherm (dots blue color) and the Freundlich isotherm (dashed red color) for the adsorption of DA ions on the MSNPs/OPA-2ME at optimum conditions.

(a) durability of the developed sensor over 12 months, (b) relationship between the emission intensity and the time after preparation, (c) Schematic diagram for detection of extracellular DA release from PC12 cells by using MSNPs/OPA/2ME, (d) emission spectra of DA released from three PC12 cells samples after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME, and (e) emission spectra of the PBS incubated with MCF7 cells after interacting with MSNPs/OPA/2ME.
Schematic diagram for detection of DA by using MSNPs/OPA/2ME.
Comparing the efficiency of the developed DA sensor with the reported sensors.

row.entry: dual-emission fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles
Dopamine modified Carbon NP with Fe+3

head.ce:title: A photoluminescence sensor for in-situ monitoring of the dopamine neurotransmitters released from PC12 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Jeddah, P.O. Box 80327, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical & Biomolecular Engineering, Sogang University, #1 Shinsu-dong, Mapo-gu, Seoul 121-742, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Assiut University, Assiut 71516, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Jeddah, P.O. Box 80327, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Constructing simple, stable, fast, and sensitive neurotransmitter-based sensors is a promising tool to diagnose neurological diseases. Dopamine (DA), “a catecholamine neurotransmitter” is important in transmitting nerve impulses. Therefore, great attention is taken to monitor DA concentrations received. The challenge in developing a DA-based sensor is to enhance its stability and sensitivity. Thus, we have used o-phthalaldehyde (OPA)/2-mercapto ethanol (2ME)/mesoporous silica instated of 2ME in solution. Here we have successfully developed a fluorescence DA neurotransmitters sensor. The sensor was used for detecting a wide range of concentrations of DA (5 nM to 5 µM). Effects of pH (4.3–11.4) and temperatures (25–70 °C) on the sensor efficiency were investigated. The detection limit was 1.35 × 10−11 mol/dm3, which is lower than the normal DA level in the central nervous system. The results indicated that using OPA/2ME/MSNPs has long-time stability over a year of its preparation. Moreover, the developed sensor showed high specificity towards DA in the presence of different interferences such as ascorbic acid or another catecholamine neurotransmitter such as γ-aminobutyric acid. Finally, the fabricated biosensor was used to monitor the DA neurotransmitter released from PC12 cells. Hence, it was successfully developed a simple and stable probe for accurate photoluminescence detection of DA neurotransmitters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) neurotransmitter plays an important role in many biological systems [1]. The abnormal DA concentration is a sign of different diseases such as Parkinson's, Huntington's, and schizophrenia [1–4]. Several electrochemical [5–9], optical [10–12], spectroscopic [12–14], and chromatography-based techniques [15–21] were reported for DA determination. While the spectroscopic methods showed a very low detection limit. However, these methods needed sample pretreatment, a long time for analysis, and expensive equipment. The electrochemical-based methods for neurotransmitter detection are simple, rapid response, easy, and high sensitivity methods [5]. But the existence of ascorbic acid and uric acid that could oxidize at almost the same potential as DA is an obstacle to the wide uses of the electrochemical method [22–29]. Therefore, developing a rapid, cheap, sensitive, and specific technique for DA detection in biological samples is still needed.

Recently, several colorimetric sensors have been reported for DA determination depending on the color changes of the gold nanoparticles [30–36]. Au NPs were modified with different chemicals or DNA aptamers that could interact with the DA molecules. Furthermore, carbon dots modified Au NPs were also used as a probe for colorimetric DA determination. Moreover, Au-Ag core–shell nanostructures were reported as DA colorimetric sensors [30]. However, there is a need for developing more cheaper materials that could be applicable for commercial uses.

A mixture of o-phthalaldehyde (OPA)/thiol compound has been widely reported as a derivatizing agent for the detection of γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) based on the reaction with the amino groups to generate electro and fluorescent active derivatives [37,38]. Recently, we have reported the fabrication of an optical GABA-based UV–vis assay in the solution based on interacting with different thiol compounds andOPA [39]. It was found that 16-mercaptohexadecanoic acid has formed the most stable GABA response. Although this assay shows the ability to detect low concentrations of GABA in the aqueous solution. However, the use of long-chain thiol compounds could affect the cells' viability. Moreover, there is a need for developing material that could be applied for real-time monitoring of the DA release.

The porous silica NPs are nanoscale and powerful frameworks for many technical applications due to their unique advantages including high stability, low cost, and large surface area, which are important aspects of powerful sensors [40–44]. The PC12 cell line was used as an in vitro model for dopaminergic neurons, which could release measurable DA amounts in response to compounds such as Cisplatin and glucocorticoids. Here, we reported a simple, cheap, stable, and rapid optical sensor for detecting DA based on fluorescence technique with long-term stability. The sensing process includes firstly the formation of a thioacetal compound (2-mercapto ethanol (2ME)/OPA) inside the pores of the mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSNPS). Second, the interaction between the thioacetal compound and the amino group of DA moiety resulted in forming a yellow-colored isoindole compound (Scheme 1 ). Then, UV–vis and fluorescence techniques were used for monitoring this isoindole compound. The effects of a wide range of pH on the detection of DA were examined. Also, the detection of different DA concentrations mixed with ascorbic acid (AA) in human serum samples was investigated as representative of real sample applications. Furthermore, the present sensor showed high specificity toward the different catecholamine neurotransmitters. The results indicate that a low detection limit of DA was obtained. Also, the MSNPs-OPA sensor can be a promising tool for detecting and screening neurotransmitters. Finally, the applicability of this sensor for monitoring DA released by PC12 cells was confirmed. The proposed sensor showed a promising potential to monitor neurotransmitter detection in a short period with high stability.

DA, AA, OPA, 2ME, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), human serum, cetrimonium bromide (CTAB), and tetramethyl orthosilicate (TMOS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) and antibiotics were obtained from Gibco (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Deionized water (DIW) (18.2 MΩ.cm) was used for preparing all aqueous solutions. A Perkin Elmer UV/Vis Spectrophotometer was used to measure the absorbance spectra.

MSNPS were synthesized at a low cost using a sol–gel technique in the presence of CTAB surfactant as a directing agent. 5.0 g NaOH and 0.2 g CTAB were dissolved in 100 mL DIW. Then 6 g TMOS was added to the previous mixture and stirred at 25 °C for about 6 h until a gelatinous white precipitate was formed. After that, the precipitate was washed with DIW/ethanol before drying for 6 h at 85 °C. Finally, the MSNPs were calcinated at 550 °C for 3 h to remove the CTAB residual.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the optical DA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The DA optical sensor MSNS-OPA/2ME was fabricated via the anchor approach. Firstly, 1 gm of MSNS was suspended in 40 mL ethanol and then mixed with 5 mg of OPA and 2 µL of 2ME. OPA/2ME was steadied inside the MSNPs' pores using a slight vacuum attached to a rotary at 50 °C. Finally, ethanol was removed at 65 °C and formed a pale-yellow precipitate after washing with DIW and dried for 2 h at 60 °C.

The PC12 cells and MCF7 breast cancer cells were cultured (1 × 105 cells/mL) in DMEM media containing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics. The cells were maintained in the incubator at 37 °C under a 5 % CO2 atmosphere, and the media was refreshed every-two days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA sensing and real sample application
ce:section.ce:para: The wanted DA concentrations were prepared in a total volume of 5 mL PBS. Then 0.1 gm of MSNS-OPA/ME was used to monitor different concentrations of DA shaking for 5 min. The MSNS-OPA/ME/DA was kept at room temperature until the measurement time. The UV–vis and emission spectra were performed. The developed sensor was used for detecting DA release from different PC12 cell samples. MCF7 cell line was used as a negative control.

Scheme 1 represented the synthetic route of the MSNPs-OPA/ME optical sensor, which included the synthesis of MSNPs based on the sol–gel process using CTAB as a directing agent. The presence of the porous structures could work as a carrier for the OPE/2ME for the synthesis of MSNPS-OPA/2ME as an optical sensor for DA released by PC12 cells. The morphology of the MSNPs was studied using SEM and TEM techniques. Fig. 1
a demonstrated the formation of highly uniform nanospheres of the same size and morphology. The average size of the MSNPs was about 40 nm.

Furthermore, Fig. 1 b shows the TEM image of the silica nanospheres that indicated the presence of uniform pores in these MSNPs. These data imply that the MSNPs have a mesoporous structure, which is one of the advantages of using MSNPs as a carrier sensor. The pores were used for the accommodation of the OPE/2ME thioacetal probes inside the MSNPs.

The MSNPs-OPA/2ME optical sensor was fabricated by treating the MSNPs with OPA/2ME (1 %) as a probe that could interact with DA neurotransmitters. The sensing process of the DA is based on color changes, and it could be evaluated based on the UV–vis and photoluminescence techniques. The functionalization of MSNPs with OPA/2ME was confirmed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1 c). The FTIR spectrum of the free-surface MSNPs (Fig. 1 c, black curve) displayed several characteristic bands. Absorption band 3000–3500 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of both Si–OH and –OH groups of the physisorbed water molecules. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum also showed a band at 1062 cm−1 (Si–O–Si stretching vibration), strong bands at 801 cm−1 and 482 cm−1, which are assigned to the tetrahedron ring of the [SiO4]- and rocking of (Si–O–Si), respectively [45]. Furthermore, the spectrum of the MSNPs-OPA/2ME (Fig. 1 c, red curve) displayed a similar spectrum of free MSNPs with two additional bands at 1640 and 1720 cm−1, which confirmed the formation of the thioacetal inside the pores of MSNPs.

After confirming the formation of the MSNPs modified with OPA/2ME composite, the capability of this composite as a probe for detecting DA neurotransmitters based on the photoluminescence technique was studied. A 100 µg of MSNPs/OPA/2ME was mixed with different concentrations of DA solutions for 2 min at room temperature, and then the sensor was filtered off. The amount of DA could assessment qualitatively determined using the naked eye based on the degrees of color change. While the quantitative assessment was performed using a solid UV–vis spectrophotometer and solid spectrofluorometer instruments.

First, the ability of MSNPs for detecting a solution of DA in PBS (4 µmol/L) was investigated. Fig. 2
a (black curve) showed that no absorption band could be observed in the UV–vis spectrum of MSNPs in the presence of DA. This result illustrated that MSNPs could not be directly used for DA detection. On the other hand, the interaction of MSNPs/OPA-2ME with DA results in the color change of the MSNPs/OPA-2ME from white to pale yellow. In addition, the UV–vis spectrum of MSNPs/OPA-2ME/DA showed an absorption band at 380 nm (Fig. 2 a red curve), which confirmed the ability of MSNPs/OPA-2ME to detect DA neurotransmitters. The capturing of the DA by MSNPs/OPA-2ME probe was also confirmed using the Raman spectroscopy technique. Fig. 2 b showed the Raman spectrum of MSNPs/OPA-2ME after interacting with DA that displayed a set of strong bands, including the following characteristics bands [46,47]: 1480 cm−1 (phenyl CC stretches), 750 cm−1 (CH out-of-plane, and ring deformation (two band response)), 962 cm−1 (NH twisting, CH wagging and ring deformation), 1060 cm−1 (CCN stretching and CH wagging), 1118.5 cm−1 (CH twist, CN, stretching and NH twist), 1146 cm−1 (OH rocking, and CH aromatic rocking), 1285 cm−1 (CH twisting), 1319 cm−1 (CH aromatic in-plane rocking and CH twisting), 1450 cm−1 (CH scissoring), and 1617 cm−1 (Ring deformation).

pH value is one of the important parameters, which could affect the interaction between DA and the OPA/2ME probe beside the effect of pH on the DA itself. Fig. 2 c showed the effects of different pHs (4.3–11.4) on the interaction of DA with MSNPs/OPA-2ME, demonstrating different absorption bands based on the solution’s pH. The intensities of the absorption bands were also increased by changing the pH value from the acidic medium to the alkaline medium. In addition, a new absorption band was observed at 550 nm in the case of the alkaline medium that could relate to the instability of DA in the alkaline medium, which can easily oxidize and convert it to polydopamine [48]. Fig. 2 d shows the photo images of DA after interacting with MSNPs/OPA-2ME at different pH values. Thus, all further experiments were achieved in PBS (pH, 7.4). Furthermore, the behavior of DA under different pH was also studied (Fig. 2 e). The results showed the change of the DA color into a black color in the presence of an alkaline medium, which could be related to the self-polymerization of DA.

Moreover, the temperature effects (25–70 °C) on DA detection using the MSNPs/OPA/2ME sensor were investigated (Fig. 2 f). The results show an increase in the absorption band intensity at 380 nm with increasing temperature (25–50 °C). Furthermore, an additional absorption band at 468 nm was also observed. At 70 °C, only a strong absorption band was observed at 398 nm, and two absorption bands at 380 nm and 468 nm disappeared. These results indicate the polymerization of DA at high temperatures. Thus, we have selected 30 °C as an optimum temperature for detecting DA, especially for PC12 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and specificity of dopamine neurotransmitter monitoring using MSNPs/OPA/2ME sensor based on UV–vis technique
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3
a showed the UV–vis spectra of 100 mg of MSNPs/OPA/2ME sensor after interaction with different DA concentrations (5 nmol/L – 50 µmol/L). The results illustrated an increase in the intensity of the absorption band at 380 nm with increasing the DA concentration. Fig. 3 b represents the relationship between the DA concentrations in the range of 50 nmol/L –5 µmol/L. The absorption band intensities at 380 nm displayed a linear curve with an R2 of 0.983. The limit of detection (LOD) of the DA based on UV–vis was calculated using equation (1), and it was found to be as low as 40 nmol/L.
(1)
LOD
= 3.3 ∗
STEYX/Slope of calibration curve

Although the developed UV-based sensor showed good sensitivity, there is still a need for developing a more sensitive sensor. Ascorbic acid (AA) is among the most co-existence interference in biological DA samples and could greatly influence thedetection of DA molecules. Therefore, it is important to study the capability of our sensor for detecting DA in the existence of AA as interference. To study the interference of AA with DA, the developed sensor was used for detecting different DA concentrations in the presence of 3 µM AA. Fig. 3 c showed an absorption band at 380 nm that related to the interaction between DA with the MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor without any interference effect for the AA.

The specification of the developed sensor was investigated by studying the response of this sensor to another neurotransmitter “GABA” compared to the DA neurotransmitter. Fig. 3 d (red curve) showed the UV–vis spectrum of the MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor after interaction with GABA that showed an absorption band at 334 nm, which is lower than the absorption wavelength of the DA@MSNPs/OPA-2ME “i.e., 380 nm” (Fig. 3 d (black curve)). This difference in the absorption wavelength is related to the difference in the chemical structure of DA and GABA. The longer wavelength in the case of DA could be related to the presence of the benzene ring.

To extend the application of the developed sensor for detecting other catecholamine neurotransmitters, MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor was used for detecting 10 µmol/L of norepinephrine (NE) as shown in Fig. 3 d (blue curve). The absorbance spectrum of the E neurotransmitter showed an emission band at about 395 nm. This longer wavelength could be related to the presence of one more hydroxyl group in the NE compound compared with the DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence determination of DA using MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
It was reported that the normal concentration of the DA in the central nervous system (CNS) is about 100 nM, thus the development of a highly sensitive sensor for monitoring any abnormal change in the DA concentration. Therefore, due to its high sensitivity, the photoluminescence technique was used to detect DA instead of the UV–vis spectroscopy technique. Fig. 4
a showed the photoluminescence emission spectra of 100 mg of MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor after interacting with different DA concentrations (5 nmol/L –5 µmol/L). The results showed an emission band at 430 nm. Furthermore, the emission band intensity was increased with increasing the concentration of DA. The uses of this concentration-wide range demonstrated a saturation of the emission at the DA high concentrations (Fig. 4 b). The relationship between the emission band intensities and the DA concentrations in the range of 5 nmol/L to 50 nmol/L was investigated (Fig. 4 b, inset). Fig. 4 c shows photo images of the MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor before and after interaction with different concentrations of DA (5 nmol/L to 5 µmol/L), which showed the capability of detecting the presence of DA at low concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The limits of detection of DA neurotransmitters and the interaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the interaction of DA with MSNPs/OPA-2ME were determined according to equation (2) [49]:
(2)
LODor
( L O Q )
=
K
S b
l

where Sb is the standard deviation, l is the slope of the calibration curve, and the constant K is equal to 3 and 10 for LOD and LOQ, respectively. The linear correlation values ranged from 5 × 10−9 to 5 × 10−8 M. The LOD and LOQ were 1.35 × 10−11 mol/dm3 and 7.2 × 10−10 mol/dm3, respectively. The LOD is lower than that in the case of the UV–vis technique. which indicated the sensitive sensing of nanomolar concentrations of DA. The sensitivity of the developed DA sensor was compared with the previously reported sensors based on either UV–vis or fluorescence spectroscopy. The results were listed in Table 1 , which showed that the current DA sensor has high sensitivity compared with most of the recently reported DA sensors.

The effect of DA initial concentrations on capturing the MSNPs/OPA-2ME was calculated by changing the DA concentrations at the specified optimal conditions. The adsorption of DA on MSNPs/OPA-2ME increases sharply until reaching saturation at higher DA concentrations. The maximum capacity of MSNPs/OPA-2ME reached 1780 nM/g at 25 °C ( Fig. 4 d). To describe the interaction mechanism on MSNPs/OPA-2ME surface, the equilibrium/isotherm parameters were determined for the MSNPs/OPA-2ME using Langmuir, Freundlich, and Langmuir–Freundlich adsorption models. The modified Langmuir-Freundlich model [49] provided an excellent fitting to describe the interaction mechanism as the value of correlation coefficients (R2 ) was > 0.99 according to equation (3):
(3)
q e
=
QK
C
e
n
1 + K
C
e
n

qe and Q are the experimental and calculated maximum sorption capacity on MSNPs/OPA-2ME (nM/g), respectively. While K and n are equilibrium concentration constants related to the affinity coefficient and adsorption energy, respectively.

According to the very close values to the determination coefficients, the isotherm fits the Langmuir-Freundlich model, which is almost superimposed instead of the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.7092). The maximum sorption capacity determined with the Langmuir-Freundlich model (1836.7 nM/g) is closer to the experimental value qe (1780 nM/g). The value of n (6.57) is more significant than unity indicating the chemical interaction [50,51]. Isotherms with n > 1 are classified as l-type isotherms reflecting a high affinity between DA and MSNPs/OPA-2ME surface and are indicative of chemisorption (Fig. 4 d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The durability of the developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The durability of the developed sensor was investigated for over a year. Fig. 5
a showed the emission spectra of the as-prepared MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor after interaction with the DA in comparison with its response after different periods of preparation. The emission spectra showed a band at 430 nm and the intensity of the emission bands was slightly decreased over a year of preparation. Fig. 5 b showed the relationship between the emission band and the period of preparation, which showed that the developed sensor retained>92 % of its sensitivity after a year of preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of dopamine released from PC12 cells as a model of real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, monitoring of DA released from PC12 cell have witnessed tremendous progress in cell biology and toxicology research [52]. The capability of the developed sensor for monitoring DA neurotransmitters released from PC12 cells was investigated (Fig. 5 c). The emission spectra of three samples of PC12 that contain different numbers of PC12 cells (0.7, 1.1, and 1.4 × 105 cells/mL) were represented in Fig. 5 d. The results showed an emission band at 457 nm, which is located at a longer wavelength than chemical DA in PBS (430 nm). This difference in the emission wavelength is attributed to the presence of several species in the extracellular matrix. The intensity of the emission band depends on the density of the PC12 cells.

To confirm the specificity of the developed sensor for the in-situ DA detection, MCF7 cells “a cancer cell line”, which couldn't release DA was used as a negative control. Fig. 5 e Showed the emission spectrum of PBS after incubation with MCF7 cells (1.2 × 105 cells/mL), the fluorescence spectrum didn't show any emission peak. Hence, the developed sensor is specific for the detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters. These results confirmed the capability of the MSNPs/OPA-2ME sensor to apply for monitoring DA released from PC12 cells without any interference effect. Also, the developed sensor showed high stability over a year.

The present work demonstrates the fabrication of a long durability neurotransmitters optical biosensor based on a simple fluorescence technique by using OPA/2ME as a probe for the interaction with DA molecules. The effects of different conditions, including temperature, pH value, and interference on the detection of DA were investigated. The developed sensor showed good sensitivity, specificity, and long-term stability advantages. The PC12 cells were used as a model of the dopaminergic neuronal cell line that could release DA. The synthesized probe has been applied successfully for detecting the real-time released DA neurotransmitter from PC12 cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work has been funded by the Deputyship for Research& Innovation, Ministry of Education in Saudi Arabia through project number MOE-IF-20-03-1. Therefore, the authors gratefully acknowledge technical and financial support from the Ministry of Education and the University of Jeddah. This research was supported by the Brain Pool program funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT through the National Research Foundation of Korea (171S-4-3-1800).

S.R. Ali, Y. Ma, R.R. Parajuli, Y. Balogun, W.Y.C. Lai, H. He, Anal. Chem. 2007, 79, 2583.
P. Damier, E.C. Hirsch, Y. Agid, A.M. Graybiel, Brain, 1999, 122, 1437.
W.Z.W. Ismail,G. Liu,K. Zhang,E.M. Goldys,M.J. Dawes, Opt. Express,2016, 24, 85-94.
Y.-R. Kim, S. Bong, Y.-J. Kang, Y. Yang, R.K. Mahajan, J.S. Kim, H. Kim, Biosens. Bioelectron. 2010, 25, 2366.
J.W. Mo, B. Ogorevc, Anal. Chem. 2001, 73, 1196–1202.
H. Shifeng, M.L. Kasner, S. Shujun, P. Krutika, C. Robert, J. Phys. Chem. C, 2010, 114, 14915–14921.
